perturbation.original_text: Within 10 days of identifying a Business Source, Monnit shall amend Exhibit A to include such Business Source and deliver a copy of the amended Exhibit A to Receiving Party. The Receiving Party shall have 2 business days to object to the amendment to Exhibit A by providing documentation that evidences Receiving Party's prior relationship with the Business Source added to Exhibit A.
perturbation.changed_text: Within 10 days of identifying a Business Source, Monnit shall amend Exhibit A to include such Business Source and deliver a copy of the amended Exhibit A to Receiving Party. The Receiving Party shall have 1 business day to object to the amendment to Exhibit A by providing documentation that evidences Receiving Party's prior relationship with the Business Source added to Exhibit A.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the objection period to one business day may be insufficient under general contract law principles, particularly in complex transactions, because it doesn't provide reasonable time to review documentation. This timeline could be deemed unreasonable, potentially leading to unenforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Principles of Contract Law - Reasonable Time
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification reduces the time to object to one day, which may be deemed unreasonable under Utah law, particularly Section 70A-1-304, which implies that actions must be taken within a reasonable time. Determining whether one day is reasonable is up to the Court to decide.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the objection period from two business days to one. While the provided law snippets are missing, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest that this change may be deemed unreasonable under Utah law, specifically referencing implied reasonable timeframes. The original text provided two days; reducing that to one could be seen as creating a potentially unenforceable situation, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of circumvention by any of the undersigned Parties, whether direct and/or indirect, the circumvented Party shall be entitled to a legal monetary compensation equal to ten times the maximum service fees it would have realized from such a transaction, plus any and all expenses, including any and all legal fees incurred in lieu of the recovery of such compensation.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of circumvention by any of the undersigned Parties, whether direct and/or indirect, the circumvented Party shall be entitled to a legal monetary compensation equal to twenty times the maximum service fees it would have realized from such a transaction, plus any and all expenses, including any and all legal fees incurred in lieu of the recovery of such compensation.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the damages multiplier to twenty times the service fees raises concerns about whether it constitutes an unenforceable penalty rather than a reasonable estimate of actual damages. Utah courts generally disfavor punitive damages in contract cases and might find such a high multiplier to be an impermissible penalty.
perturbation.law_explanation: Modifying the compensation to twenty times the maximum service fees may constitute an unenforceable penalty under general contract law principles and as described in the Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 356. Utah courts, like most, typically do not enforce penalty clauses in contracts, especially if the amount is grossly disproportionate to the actual harm suffered. For sales of goods, this is codified in Utah's adoption of UCC § 2-718 (covered via the URL). The 20x multiplier may be seen as a penalty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: liquidated damages | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you liquidated damages Liquidated damages are an exact amount of money, or a set formula to calculate the amount of money, a party will owe if it breaches a contract , in order to compensate the injured party for its losses. Liquidated damages must be clearly stated in a section or clause of a contract and agreed upon by the parties prior to entering a contract. Liquidated damages are a variety of actual damages and a remedy for breach of contract. Parties to a contract use liquidated damages where actual damages, though real, are difficult or impossible to prove. Liquidated damages provide a clear monetary value to compensate the injured party while saving time and resources on litigation determining compensatory damages . Liquidated damages can also be used to deter parties from breaching contracts. Legal disputes surrounding liquidated damages arise following a breach of contract when either the injured party believes the liquidated damages are not adequately compensatory or the breaching party believes the liquidated damages are unreasonably high. Courts will not impose liquidated damages if the clause is punitive , illegal , unconscionable , or contrary to public policy . Example Undisclosed source code has value as a trade secret . Openly publishing the source code destroys the trade secret value. The trade secret value of source code is difficult or impossible to prove. There is no open market for "secret source code". In the 1997 equity case of Sun Microsystems, Inc. vs. Microsoft , the plaintiff Sun Microsystems made several damage claims. One was for $35 million in liquidated damages for Java source code disclosure, a contract violation. The case was later settled on terms, including a $20 million payment by Microsoft to Sun. [Last reviewed in June of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS courts legal practice/ethics business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure commercial transactions legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 2-718. Liquidation or Limitation of Damages; Deposits. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-718. Liquidation or Limitation of Damages; Deposits. (1) Damages for breach by either party may be liquidated in the agreement but only at an amount which is reasonable in the light of the anticipated or actual harm caused by the breach, the difficulties of proof of loss, and the inconvenience or nonfeasibility of otherwise obtaining an adequate remedy. A term fixing unreasonably large liquidated damages is void as a penalty. (2) Where the seller justifiably withholds delivery of goods because of the buyer's breach, the buyer is entitled to restitution of any amount by which the sum of his payments exceeds (a) the amount to which the seller is entitled by virtue of terms liquidating the seller's damages in accordance with subsection (1), or (b) in the absence of such terms, twenty per cent of the value of the total performance for which the buyer is obligated under the contract or $500, whichever is smaller. (3) The buyer's right to restitution under subsection (2) is subject to offset to the extent that the seller establishes (a) a right to recover damages under the provisions of this Article other than subsection (1), and (b) the amount or value of any benefits received by the buyer directly or indirectly by reason of the contract . (4) Where a seller has received payment in goods their reasonable value or the proceeds of their resale shall be treated as payments for the purposes of subsection (2); but if the seller has notice of the buyer's breach before reselling goods received in part performance, his resale is subject to the conditions laid down in this Article on resale by an aggrieved seller (Section 2-706 ). Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification to increase the compensation to twenty times the maximum service fees creates a legal contradiction because it potentially violates the prohibition against unenforceable penalties. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both suggest that such a high multiplier may be deemed a penalty, especially if it is grossly disproportionate to the actual harm suffered. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that "Courts will not impose liquidated damages if the clause is punitive, illegal, unconscionable, or contrary to public policy." Similarly, Scraped Law Snippet 2 notes that "A term fixing unreasonably large liquidated damages is void as a penalty." By increasing the multiplier, the changed text risks being classified as an unenforceable penalty, thus contradicting the principle that liquidated damages must be a reasonable estimate of actual harm.

perturbation.original_text: Monnit will identify certain Business Sources not known by Receiving Party which shall be set forth on Exhibit A, attached hereto and incorporated herein. Within 10 days of identifying a Business Source, Monnit shall amend Exhibit A to include such Business Source and deliver a copy of the amended Exhibit A to Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Monnit will identify certain Business Sources not known by Receiving Party which shall be set forth on Exhibit A, attached hereto and incorporated herein. Within 60 days of identifying a Business Source, Monnit shall amend Exhibit A to include such Business Source and deliver a copy of the amended Exhibit A to Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the timeframe to amend Exhibit A to 60 days could be considered unreasonable and violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, potentially harming the receiving party and delaying their business operations, violating Utah law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification extends the deadline for Monnit to update Exhibit A to 60 days, which could be seen as a violation of the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. While there is no single statute on this topic, the Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 205 (accessed through the Cornell Law Wex link) outlines this duty, and Utah courts generally recognize it. Delaying the provision of updated information for such an extended period could unfairly hinder the Receiving Party's ability to conduct business.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The change from 10 days to 60 days to amend Exhibit A introduces an inconsistency that violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines good faith as encompassing 'honest dealing' and 'observance of fair dealing standards.' Extending the deadline significantly could hinder the Receiving Party's business operations and be seen as not acting in good faith. This falls under the definition of Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction, where a time-sensitive obligation in a contract does not align with legal requirements, making the terms unenforceable.
